scenario: Atlas Manufacturing is preparing a Camunda 8.8 migration plan that touches an active user task across 1200 instances of its maintenance-request process. The team needs to know what element types are allowed on each side of a single mapping instruction before they sign off on the migration plan.
question: Which constraint applies to the source and target element types within a single migration mapping instruction?

options.text: The target element must be of the same type as the source element; you cannot map an element to an element of a different type, such as a service task mapped to a user task.
The target element may be any task type as long as it has the same id as the source element; the migration tool widens task types automatically during the active element migration step.
The target element must be of a strictly broader type than the source, so service tasks may only be remapped to abstract task elements in the target model during process instance migration.
The target element type is irrelevant at migration time; the engine derives the runtime type from the mapping plan metadata rather than from the BPMN element definitions in the target.

a.text: Correct. The documentation explicitly states you cannot map an element to an element of a different type, so an active service task may only be mapped to a service task and not, for example, to a user task.

b.text: Incorrect. There is no automatic type widening across task types; mapping is restricted to elements of the same type regardless of the chosen element id.

c.text: Incorrect. The constraint is not 'strictly broader' but 'same type'; mapping across types is not permitted in either direction.

d.text: Incorrect. The target element's BPMN type is what governs the migration; the engine enforces same-type mapping rather than deriving type from plan metadata.

explanation: Migration mapping instructions must respect element type identity: a source element can only be mapped to a target element of the same type, so a service task maps to a service task and a user task to a user task.
